stem:
Let \( [[letter]]_1, [[letter]]_2, \ldots, [[letter]]_{[[n]]} \) be [[n]] propositional variables. Define \( P: 
eg([[letter]]_1 \wedge [[letter]]_2 \wedge \cdots \wedge [[letter]]_{[[n]]}) \) and \( Q: 
eg [[letter]]_1 \vee 
eg [[letter]]_2 \vee \cdots \vee 
eg [[letter]]_{[[n]]} \). Over all \( 2^{[[n]]} = [[rows]] \) possible truth-value assignments to \( [[letter]]_1,\ldots,[[letter]]_{[[n]]} \), for how many assignments do \(P\) and \(Q\) have the same truth value?
solution:
By De Morgan's law, \( 
eg([[letter]]_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge [[letter]]_{[[n]]}) \) and \( 
eg [[letter]]_1 \vee \cdots \vee 
eg [[letter]]_{[[n]]} \) are LOGICALLY EQUIVALENT, so \(P\) and \(Q\) agree on EVERY row of the truth table: all \( [[rows]] \) assignments, so the answer is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes the two statements never agree, but De Morgan's law makes them logically equivalent.
This guesses the two statements disagree on exactly \( [[n]] \) of the rows.
This assumes the statements agree on only half the rows, as if they were unrelated formulas.
This confuses the number of AGREEING rows with the number of VARIABLES.
This assumes exactly one row of disagreement rather than full agreement.
This is an arbitrary multiple of the variable count, not the row-agreement count.

stem: Let \( [[letter]]_1, [[letter]]_2, \ldots, [[letter]]_{[[n]]} \) be [[n]] propositional variables. Let \( P \) be their CONJUNCTION \( [[letter]]_1 \wedge [[letter]]_2 \wedge \cdots \wedge [[letter]]_{[[n]]} \) and let \( Q \) be their DISJUNCTION \( [[letter]]_1 \vee [[letter]]_2 \vee \cdots \vee [[letter]]_{[[n]]} \). Over all \( 2^{[[n]]} = [[rows]] \) possible truth-value assignments, for how many assignments do \(P\) and \(Q\) have the same truth value?
solution: The conjunction \(P\) is true only on the ALL-TRUE row, and the disjunction \(Q\) is false only on the ALL-FALSE row; every OTHER row has \(P\) false and \(Q\) true, so they DISAGREE there. \(P\) and \(Q\) agree only on the all-false row (both false) and the all-true row (both true): exactly \( [[answer]] \) of the \( [[rows]] \) assignments.
distractor_rationales: This assumes the conjunction and disjunction never coincide, but they always agree on the all-false and all-true rows.
This assumes only a single row agrees and forgets that BOTH the all-false and all-true rows agree.
This assumes the conjunction and disjunction are logically equivalent and always agree, which is false whenever not every row is extreme.
This is off by one from the total row count, as if all but one row agreed.
This is off by three from the total row count, an arbitrary miscount of the agreeing rows.
This is an arbitrary multiple of the variable count, not the true row-agreement count.

stem: Let \( [[letter]]_1, [[letter]]_2, \ldots, [[letter]]_{[[n]]} \) be [[n]] propositional variables. Let \( P \) be true exactly when an ODD number of \( [[letter]]_1,\ldots,[[letter]]_{[[n]]} \) are true (their XOR / parity), and let \( Q: [[letter]]_1 \vee [[letter]]_2 \vee \cdots \vee [[letter]]_{[[n]]} \) (their disjunction). Over all \( 2^{[[n]]} = [[rows]] \) possible truth-value assignments, for how many assignments do \(P\) and \(Q\) have the same truth value?
solution: \(P\) and \(Q\) agree exactly when the number of true variables is 0 (both false) or when it is odd (both true, since an odd count is at least 1). They disagree exactly when the true-count is a positive EVEN number. Counting rows: the all-false row (1 way) plus every row with an odd number of true variables (\(2^{[[n]]-1}\) of the \( [[rows]] \) rows) gives \( 1+2^{[[n]]-1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes parity and disjunction are logically equivalent and always agree, but they disagree whenever an even number \(\ge 2\) of variables are true.
This assumes they never agree, ignoring the all-false row and every odd-count row where both are true.
This assumes they agree on exactly half the rows, which overcounts the disagreement from even-but-nonzero true counts.
This confuses the number of AGREEING rows with the number of VARIABLES.
This subtracts too many rows, as if only a single row of agreement existed beyond the odd-count rows.
This is an arbitrary multiple of the variable count, not the true row-agreement count.
